caption:
5 JavaScript array methods you probably never used.

They've been in every browser since 2023. No polyfills. No libraries.

Stop writing verbose loops when a one-liner exists.

📌 SAVE this — you'll need it.

Which one surprised you? Comment 👇

#javascript #webdev #coding #programming #frontend #developer #codingtips #es2023 #arrays #cleancode

slides.title: JS Array Methods Nobody Uses
slides.subtitle: Built-in since ES2023. Zero polyfills needed.

slides.title: .findLast( ) — search from the end
slides.desc: Like .find() but starts from the last element. No more .reverse().find().
slides.punchline: No copy. No reverse. One method.

slides.title: .at( ) — negative indexing
slides.desc: Access elements from the end without arr[arr.length - 1].
slides.punchline: Python had this for 30 years. JS finally caught up.

slides.title: .with( ) — immutable replace
slides.desc: Replace an element at an index without mutating the original array.
slides.punchline: React state updates just got cleaner.

slides.title: .toSorted( ) — sort without mutating
slides.desc: .sort() mutates the original. .toSorted() returns a new array.

slides.title: .toReversed( ) — reverse without mutating
slides.desc: .reverse() mutates. .toReversed() returns a new reversed array.
slides.punchline: The immutable trio: toSorted, toReversed, with.

slides.title: ES2023 Array Cheat Sheet
slides.question: Which one are you using first? 👇

slides.teaser: Next: Iterator helpers — map and filter without arrays
